dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for reported falls at a residence in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Sarah, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My father was acting strangely. He was in his chair and seemed confused.
What kind of strange behavior? Was he in any pain or struggling to breathe?
No, he didn't seem to be in distress, but he was staring towards the left and wasn't responding well.
Did he fall at any point before this happened?
Sir, can you tell me your name and where you are right now?
Can you open your eyes and tell me what you see?
What is your name, sir?
I'm going to touch your shoulder. Can you squeeze my hand in return?
Sir, can you breathe comfortably right now?
I'll check your breathing and lung sounds to make sure there's no problem.
I'll check your pulse now to get a good reading.

dialogue.micro_intent: head_and_neck_exam - Pupillary reflex / Size of pupils
dialogue.utterance: My name is Carlos, a paramedic. I'm using a light to check your pupils. Are you okay with that?
I can see from the tissue that you have a colostomy on your right side.
You have movement in all four limbs and no obvious deformities.
Sir, when did you first notice the confusion and altered mental status?
How would you describe the symptoms you're experiencing today?
Do you have any memory of what happened this morning?
He has a history of stroke, A-fib, seizures, and he's diabetic. He's still on warfarin.
On a scale from 0 to 10, where 10 is the worst pain, how would you rate the discomfort you're feeling now?
You're not in any pain at all-just confused and not fully responding.
Pulse measured at 91 beats per minute.
Respirations are 16 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is 119/84.
Blood glucose is 151 mg/dL through a finger stick.
Oxygen saturation is 100%.
EKG shows atrial fibrillation.
We need to monitor your heart rhythm more closely. Can I place the EKG leads now?
Per hospital protocol 2-7, we'll place you on a 12-lead EKG and continue EKG monitoring.
Pulse now reads 82 beats per minute.
Still 16 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is now 137/87.
The glucose test came up 0-maybe the meter had an issue.
Oxygen saturation has dropped to 57%.
EKG still shows atrial fibrillation, with variable rate.
Pulse now reads 76 beats per minute.
BP is now 132/82.
Oxygen saturation is now up to 98%.
We need to transport you to MCV. That's your preferred hospital, right?
Carlos will be helping you onto the stair chair to get you out of the house safely.
Sir, we'll move you from the chair to the stretcher with safety straps in place.
Everyone ready? We'll transfer you to the ambulance carefully.
Transfer is ready-GCS is 15, eyes, verbal, and motor pupils are 4-4, oxygen improved to 98%.
EKG is running and your heart is showing atrial fibrillation with variable rate. Transport to MCV will take about 7 minutes.
The patient has been received by hospital staff, and we've alerted them of his current status and updates.
Yes, and he agreed to go to MCV.
We're documenting his history of stroke, A-fib, seizures, and diabetes as per family.
Pain level still 0-no discomfort or chest pain reported.
We observed some weakness earlier, but now the patient is moving independently with no incident.
Final destination is MCV, consistent with patient and family preferences.
The patient is being transported with proper restraints, no immobilization necessary.
No new changes since in the house. All assessments have remained stable.
We didn't give any medications during transport as the patient remained stable.
No signs of labored breathing or use of accessory muscles noted during assessment.
Skintone was warm and dry with no cyanosis observed.
We saw atrial fibrillation on the EKG with variable rate from 88-250 as per our 12-lead.
The patient doesn't remember the morning events, but family says he was eating breakfast just before the episode.
No further procedures were needed. The patient remained stable throughout transport.
The patient's condition is not worsening. No interventions needed before arrival.
Arrival at MCV will be via sheet transfer as per documentation in the narrative.
EKG still showing atrial fibrillation with no significant changes.
We've secured the patient on the stretcher with all restraints in place.
Medications include aspirin, warfarin, carvedilol, furosemide, and levetiracetam.
We placed the patient on cardiac monitoring as per field impression of AMS and atrial fibrillation.
The son described the patient began staring to the left and said he couldn't see during the conversation.
Blood pressure remains stable at 132/82.
Patient has a colostomy and a stoma. No reported complications from these during transport.
No drainage, obstruction, or sunken eyes noted in the ENMOT exam.
Patient's airway is patent and no intervention required.
No visible lacerations or swelling on the head.
The blood glucose repeated 0-device may have had an issue the first time.
The stair chair and stretcher transfer were smooth. No new injuries reported.
We asked what activities would make the confusion worse-patient has no memory to contribute.
No destination concerns reported. Arrival at MCV is en route.
Breathing is continuing at 16 breaths per minute with adequate oxygen.
No medications were given as there were no indications for acute intervention.
Continuous monitoring is maintaining atrial fibrillation readings.
The patient is lying flat and comfortable. Symptoms have not worsened.
Bilateral chest expansion is even, and no difficulty in breathing is observed.
No heart murmurs or extra heart sounds heard.
Patient denies chest pain, shortness of breath, and any new symptoms this morning.
Oxygen saturation is now 98%, likely an improvement with increasing pulse.
We'll keep the patient secure for the rest of the transport to MCV.
We're continuing to note the patient's use of warfarin in the records.
MCV is the confirmed hospital with no medical issues for transport.
The patient will be transferred via sheet method upon arrival.
No new issues during time in the ambulance.
We asked if confusion radiated to other areas-patient denies.
Patient had a fall two weeks ago and was treated for a brain bleed at MCV.
You mentioned the confusion wasn't painful but was unlike anything else.
You don't remember when the episode started, though family estimates about 30 minutes ago.
We'll report the expected arrival time to the hospital now.
No tenderness or rigidity in the abdominal area noted.
No new wounds or abrasions detected during the exam.
The patient is safely positioned for the transfer with the sheet method.
Transfer would be handled with staff from the critical care team now at MCV.
No additional procedures needed during the remaining transport.
Arrival is expected in about 7 minutes. No further interventions needed prior to arrival.
